title: Urine on Wool — Urease Enzyme Cold Soak, Gentle Handling
stainChemistry: Uric acid and urea in urine bind to wool's alpha-keratin protein. Wool fiber swells in warm water, which allows protein-damaging enzymes (protease) to penetrate. Ammonia odor comes from bacterial urease activity on urea. Cold water keeps wool compact and protects fiber. Urease breaks down urea without damaging wool (unlike protease). Uric acid crystals settle into wool's scales when dried.
whyThisWorks: Cold water prevents wool swelling and fiber damage. Urease-only enzyme breaks down urea (odor) without attacking wool protein. Gentle tamping (never rubbing) protects delicate wet wool. If fresh, cold water rinse often removes most stain and odor.

spottingProtocol.equipment: White cloth, cold water (50°F or colder)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Blot fresh urine with white cloth immediately. Rinse with cold water and continue blotting gently. Work from outside inward. Cold temperature keeps wool fiber compact and prevents uric acid from setting. Squeeze gently; do not wring.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Urease enzymatic cleaner, cold water, white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Mix urease-based enzymatic cleaner in cold water (use per instructions). Apply to stain or soak gently. Urease breaks down urea into ammonia and CO2, eliminating odor. Tamp very gently with white cloth — no rubbing. Let sit for 30 minutes minimum; overnight is better for old stains. Ammonia smell will fade as urea is broken down.
spottingProtocol.suggestedProducts: Nature's Miracle, Rocco & Roxie (applied in cold water)

spottingProtocol.instruction: Rinse thoroughly with cold water, squeezing gently to remove enzyme and dissolved urine. Continue until cloth comes away clean. Do not wring or agitate.
If any urine odor remains or if wool feels stiff, apply small amount of wool conditioner. This relaxes fiber and helps remove remaining odor molecules. Optional but recommended.

homeSolutions: FRESH: Rinse immediately with cold water and blot gently. This removes 70-80% of fresh urine and most odor.
WITHIN HOURS: Cold water rinse + urease soak (30 minutes to overnight) + cold rinse. Most fresh-to-aging urine responds.
DRIED/OLD: Extended urease soak overnight + cold rinse. If odor persists, professional cleaning recommended.
materialWarnings: NEVER use warm or hot water — swells wool fiber and allows protein-damaging enzymes to penetrate
NEVER use protease enzyme — will damage wool's protein structure
NEVER wring or agitate — wool breaks when wet
NEVER use chlorine bleach — irreversibly damages wool protein
escalation: If odor persists after urease enzyme treatment, or if urine has penetrated backing, stop. Professional dry cleaning is safer. Solvent-based spotting and odor treatment will address persistent problems without risking wool fiber damage.

household.name: Nature's Miracle Enzymatic Cleaner
household.note: Urease enzyme; safe for wool when used cold

household.name: Rocco & Roxie Professional Enzyme Cleaner
household.note: Urease dominant formula; works cold for wool

household.name: Wool Conditioner
household.note: Optional fiber care after enzyme treatment

professional.name: Professional Dry Cleaning Service
professional.note: Recommended for persistent odor or old stains

customerExplanation: Wool is a delicate protein fiber. We used only urease enzyme (which breaks down urea, the odor source) in cold water. Warm water would have swollen the fiber and allowed protein-damaging enzymes to penetrate. Everything was done cold and gently to protect the delicate fiber.
commonMistakes: Using warm or hot water — opens wool fiber and allows enzyme penetration; cold is essential
Using protease enzyme — damages wool protein; urease-only is safe
Wringing or agitating — wet wool breaks easily; squeeze gently only
Incomplete enzyme soak — urine needs extended time; overnight is ideal
scienceNote: Wool alpha-keratin protein structure depends on hydrogen bonds and disulfide bonds. Warm water disrupts hydrogen bonds and causes swelling. Protease cuts peptide bonds, breaking the protein chains. Urease, conversely, breaks C-N bonds in urea (NH2-CO-NH2) without touching protein. Urease produces ammonia gas (volatile) and CO2, both of which escape, eliminating odor at the molecular level. Cold water prevents swelling and protects wool structure.
callPro: If odor persists after urease treatment, professional dry cleaning is the safer choice. Solvents and professional odor treatments will address stubborn odor without risk of enzyme damaging wool fiber. This is especially important for fine wools (cashmere, merino).
solventNote: Professional dry cleaners use solvent spotting for urine on wool, not aqueous enzyme. Tetrachloroethylene (perc) removes uric acid effectively. For odor, professionals might use ozone treatment or odor-neutralizing compounds applied during solvent cleaning, avoiding aqueous enzyme altogether to prevent wool damage.
